title: Replace a Character
subtitle: r{char} — overwrite one character without entering Insert mode.
summary: r{char} replaces the character under the cursor with {char} and stays in Normal mode. Perfect for fixing a typo without an i…Esc dance.

blocks.text: Typo? Cursor on the wrong letter? {key:r}{c} swaps it for {c} and leaves you in Normal mode. No Insert-mode round-trip, no {key:Esc}. One change, one keystroke past the new character.

blocks.label: Replace the character under cursor with x

blocks.text: {key:r}{key:Enter} replaces the character with a newline — a sneaky way to break a line at the cursor without entering Insert mode.

blocks.title: Why r Doesn't Enter Insert Mode
blocks.text:
{key:r} is a *one-shot* operation: it consumes exactly one keystroke as its argument and applies it as a literal replacement. Internally it's not the same as {key:s} (substitute), which deletes the character and switches to Insert mode. {key:r} stays in Normal mode the entire time and produces a single change in the undo tree.

With a count, {key:{n}}{key:r}{c} replaces {n} characters with the same {c} repeated. Useful for `--------` style separators.

If you need to overwrite many characters with different ones, use Replace mode ({key:R}) — it's like Insert mode but each typed character overwrites instead of pushing. See [`transform.replace-mode`](#transform.replace-mode).

rows: Replace next n characters with c (repeated)
